Ideation Artifact

kagent + Flock Runtime Interfaces

Focus: type-safe orchestration for auto-spawning Kubernetes agents, with the boring already-solved parts cut away.

Date
2026-06-30
Topic
kagent-flock-runtime-seams
Mode
repo-grounded
Focus
typed Kubernetes agent lifecycle
Codebase Context Topic Axes Ranked Ideas Rejection Summary Sources & References

Codebase Context

Flock and kagent do not overlap as much as they first appear to. Flock is the typed coordination layer: artifacts carry schema, provenance, visibility, tags, correlation, and history; agents react through typed subscriptions and emit validated outputs. kagent is the Kubernetes-native runtime layer: agents are CRDs, declarative agents compile to Deployments and Services, BYO agents are deployed as user containers serving A2A on port 8080, and MCP/A2A are first-class tool and agent surfaces.

The strongest product move is therefore not to make another Kubernetes copilot. It is to make a typed artifact become the declarative reason a Kubernetes agent exists. Flock decides that ClusterIncident needs an investigator; kagent reconciles the actual runtime; the result returns as InvestigationReport, with history and visibility preserved.

Novelty check: kagent and ARK cover Kubernetes agent CRDs; Agent Sandbox covers isolated warm runtimes; Kubiya, K8sGPT, HolmesGPT, Robusta, and Akuity cover operational troubleshooting. Beluga AI is a yellow flag because it explicitly claims a blackboard-pattern agent framework with Kubernetes CRD deployment surfaces. The remaining white space is narrower and better: schema-validated blackboard events driving agent lifecycle, not merely deploying agents or chatting with clusters.

Raw Candidates
18
Survivors
7
Cut
11
Top Wedge
Typed spawner

Topic Axes

  • Typed runtime compilation
  • Execution bridge
  • Tool and permission bridge
  • Durable state bridge
  • Product wedge

Ranked Ideas

  • 1. Type-Safe Kubernetes Agent Spawner
  • 2. KAgentEngine for Flock
  • 3. Flock BYO A2A Runtime
  • 4. Blackboard MCP Server
  • 5. Visibility-to-RBAC Compiler
  • 6. Durable Typed Incident Orchestrator
  • 7. Warm Specialist Pools

1. Type-Safe Kubernetes Agent Spawner

Typed runtime compilation Confidence 86% Complexity High Novel wedge
Description
Define a bridge where a Flock subscription does not merely call an in-process agent. It reconciles a kagent Agent, SandboxAgent, or AgentHarness based on the input artifact type, tags, visibility, and desired output schema. The runtime may be ephemeral or persistent. Inputs and outputs stay Pydantic-validated Flock artifacts.
Basis
direct: Flock artifacts carry type, payload, tags, visibility, correlation, and history; kagent AgentSpec supports Declarative/BYO agents, A2A exposure, MCP tools, memory, and deployment settings.
Rationale
This is the cleanest answer to the original tingling: type-safe auto-spawning Kubernetes agents. kagent already gives the operator and runtime; Flock supplies the typed reason for spawning and the checked output contract.
Downsides
Needs careful ownership semantics: who deletes agents, how to handle stuck pods, whether a subscription creates one runtime per artifact, per correlation, or per type, and how to avoid accidental cluster churn.
First demo shape: publish ClusterIncident -> spawn/reuse investigator -> return InvestigationReport -> dashboard shows artifact graph plus kagent runtime status.

2. KAgentEngine for Flock

Execution bridge Confidence 82% Complexity Medium
Description
Implement a Flock EngineComponent that invokes a kagent A2A endpoint and materializes the result into the declared Flock output model. The pattern mirrors Flock's existing external-engine boundary: preserve the Flock agent API while delegating runtime execution outside the process.
Basis
direct: Flock's EngineComponent.evaluate() is the execution boundary; OpenClawEngine already demonstrates external response delegation. kagent declarative agents expose A2A URLs via the controller, and BYO agents serve A2A on port 8080.
Rationale
This is the smallest technical bridge. It proves that Flock can treat kagent as a remote typed execution backend before adding CRD compilation or lifecycle management.
Downsides
On its own, it is an adapter, not a new category. It must validate, repair, or reject non-conforming A2A responses or it loses Flock's main advantage.

3. Flock BYO A2A Runtime for kagent

Execution bridge Confidence 76% Complexity Medium
Description
Package a Flock workflow as a kagent BYO agent image. The container serves A2A on port 8080, accepts A2A tasks as typed input artifacts, runs the local Flock graph until idle, and returns a typed result as the A2A response.
Basis
direct: kagent BYO agents are user-provided containers deployed by kagent and expected to serve A2A; Flock can publish artifacts and run until idle while retaining artifact history and subscriptions.
Rationale
This flips idea 2. Instead of Flock calling kagent, kagent hosts Flock as a higher-order agent. It makes a Flock multi-agent workflow usable from any kagent/A2A surface.
Downsides
It risks hiding the typed blackboard behind a normal A2A agent card. The demo must expose artifact graph and validation, or it will look like a nice wrapper rather than a new capability.

4. Blackboard MCP Server for kagent

Durable state bridge Confidence 74% Complexity Low
Description
Expose Flock operations as a remote MCP server: publish typed artifact, query artifacts, summarize history, fetch graph, and attach evidence to a correlation. Register it in kagent as a RemoteMCPServer so kagent agents can participate in the blackboard without being rewritten.
Basis
direct: Flock already has REST artifact APIs and filtered store queries. kagent has RemoteMCPServerSpec with protocol, URL, headers, TLS, allowed namespaces, and discovered tools.
Rationale
This is a low-friction bridge and a good development wedge: kagent agents get a typed shared workspace as a tool, while Flock remains the source of artifact truth.
Downsides
MCP bridges are now common. It becomes special only if the tools enforce schemas, visibility, correlation, and replay instead of becoming generic CRUD over artifacts.

5. Visibility-to-RBAC Compiler

Tool and permission bridge Confidence 69% Complexity High
Description
Compile Flock visibility and context-provider policy into kagent/Kubernetes boundaries: namespace placement, service accounts, allowed namespaces, MCP allowed headers, required tool approvals, and optional per-tenant RemoteMCPServer references.
Basis
direct: Flock enforces visibility before context reaches engines; kagent has namespace attachment controls, service account config, MCP header propagation controls, and per-tool approval metadata.
Rationale
This is the hardening layer that makes type-safe spawning enterprise-plausible. It answers "the artifact says tenant A; did we also constrain the actual cluster runtime?"
Downsides
This can easily drift into governance theater. It should be built as a compiler that emits concrete manifests and diffs, not as a policy dashboard.
Boundary: do not lead with this. Use it as the trust story after the typed spawner demo works.

6. Durable Typed Incident Orchestrator

Product wedge Confidence 66% Complexity Medium
Description
Build a concrete incident workflow: cluster events, alerts, logs, traces, runbook attempts, proposed remediations, approvals, and final reports are all typed artifacts. kagent specialists run inside Kubernetes; Flock records the workflow and decides the next specialist from artifact state.
Basis
external: auto-remediation and troubleshooting are crowded by Kubiya, K8sGPT, HolmesGPT, Robusta, Akuity, and similar tools. reasoned: typed replayable artifacts are still a distinct operator value if they make evidence, action, approval, and output schemas explicit.
Rationale
This is the most legible demo domain. Operators instantly understand "why did the agent do that?" if every step is an artifact with a schema and correlation id.
Downsides
Generic Kubernetes AI remediation is not unique. The product copy must be "typed, replayable orchestration" rather than "AI fixes your cluster."

7. Warm Specialist Pools

Typed runtime compilation Confidence 61% Complexity High
Description
Use Flock's pending artifacts, schedules, and historical fan-out to prewarm kagent agents or substrate-backed harnesses for likely next steps. For example, a burst of LogAnomaly artifacts can warm investigator agents before downstream joins are ready.
Basis
direct: Flock has schedules, typed history, and pending execution state; kagent has deployments, sandbox agents, harnesses, and substrate concepts for ready environments.
Rationale
This turns the blackboard into a capacity planning signal. It is not just autoscaling a queue; it is warming the particular specialist runtime that a typed workflow is likely to need.
Downsides
Hard to demo cleanly and easy to reduce to KEDA-style scaling. It belongs after the lifecycle bridge proves that typed artifacts can drive runtime creation.

Rejection Summary

# Idea Reason Rejected
1Just deploy Flock on KubernetesGeneric packaging; no new coordination primitive.
2Generic AI DevOps CopilotCrowded by Kubiya, K8sGPT, HolmesGPT, Robusta, Akuity, and similar products.
3Workflow DAG engine on KubernetesArgo/Tekton and ARK workflow delegation already cover the obvious DAG layer.
4Kubernetes-native agent CRDsAlready core to kagent and ARK.
5Agent pods or sandboxed runtimesAgent Sandbox and kagent Agent Substrate already cover much of the runtime substrate.
6Autoscale agents by queue lengthKEDA-style scaling is generic; only worth revisiting when the queue item is a typed artifact contract.
7Chat UI over kagentkagent already has UI and A2A surfaces.
8Agent memory storekagent already has memory design/runtime work; Flock's stronger role is typed provenance and replay.
9MCP tool marketplaceMCP catalogs are becoming table stakes; the distinctive angle is schema-checked blackboard tools.
10OpenAPI-as-tool integrationNo strong kagent code path found in the scout pass, but as a standalone idea it is not central to the Flock/kagent fit.
11Full FlockBoard CRD suite firstInteresting, but too large and Beluga-adjacent. Start with artifact-triggered kagent lifecycle before designing a whole CRD universe.

Sources & References

  • Flock Artifact model and BlackboardStore/filter surface.
  • Flock EngineComponent, OpenClaw external-engine precedent, and context-provider visibility enforcement.
  • Flock Dapr-backed blackboard store and artifact REST API.
  • kagent Agent, BYO, A2A, tools, and memory types, plus RemoteMCPServer and AgentHarness.
  • kagent API reference, kagent A2A agent example, kagent agent memory, and kagent Agent Substrate.
  • ARK core concepts, ARK CRDs, and ARK workflows via Argo Workflows.
  • Kubernetes Agent Sandbox blog and Agent Sandbox overview.
  • Kubiya overview, Kubiya workers, Kubiya MCP, K8sGPT, and HolmesGPT CNCF post.
  • Beluga AI docs, used as a prior-art caution for blackboard-pattern agents with Kubernetes deployment surfaces.
